完全自動で実行し月100万円を稼ぐ　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　

これまじで驚いた海外の事例
85歳のおばあちゃんのスペック

・Premiere
cases.content:
85歳のおばあちゃんが
OpenClawとHermes Agentを使って
案件獲得から動画編集まで
完全自動で実行し月100万円を稼ぐ　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　

これまじで驚いた海外の事例
85歳のおばあちゃんのスペック

・Premiere Proも知らない。
・CapCutも触ったことがない。
・AIもよくわからない。

でもやったことはシンプル。
Hermes Agentをマネージャーにして、
OpenClawを作業員にした。

おばあちゃんは素材動画を入れて、
・ショート動画用に編集して
と指示するだけ。

Hermes Agentが構成を考える。
どこを使うか判断する。
テロップやサムネの方向性を決める。
投稿文まで整理する。

OpenClawが実際の作業を進める。
素材を整理する。
カット指示を作る。
テロップ案を出す。
投稿用の文章まで準備する。

おばあちゃんは最後に確認するだけ。
これで、今まで1本数時間かかっていた動画編集が、確認込みで30分ほどになった。

そして、動画編集単体ではなく、
ショート動画制作、サムネ、
投稿文、簡単な改善レポートまでセットにして提供。

月20本40万円のプランを3社。
ここの案件獲得は、OpenClawと
Hermes Agentの動画編集とは
別のエージェントが実行。

この結果月100万以上の
売り上げを達成。

Hermes Agentに管理させて、
OpenClawに実行させて、
人間は最後に判断する。

まずはOpenClawから触って
Hermes Agentも入れていこう！！

cases.content:
Seedance 2.0はプロンプトにかなり忠実なので、難しい部分もあるのですが、

例えば『2人の人物をセリフ分けする方法』などは、かなり簡単です。

添付の動画がサンプルなのですが、プロンプトを共有するとこんな感じです。

=====================
男A「シーダンスがついに使えるようになったぞ!!」
男B「まじで?!早速使ってみよう!」
男A「おー!!動画のクオリティが高すぎる!」
男AB同時に「これで映画が作れるぞー!!!!!」
=====================

男A・Bと区別して「　」でセリフを指定してあげるだけで、こんな動画が作れてしまいます。

キャラの一貫性も勝手に保ってくれるので、台本さえあれば、AIショート動画なども簡単に作ることができますね。

ちなみにSeedanceは今や画像も添付することができるので、人物のビジュアルも簡単に指定できます。

他のサンプルや詳細はYouTubeにUPしたので、コメント欄からご確認ください。

cases.title:
これは根深い問題。そもそも日本では「肖像権」も「パブリシティ権」も実は法律に明文規定がないんですよね。

長年の判例の積み重ねで確立されてきた権利ですが生成AIによる侵害は事例が新しすぎて、判例がほとんど積み上がっていません。結果として「明らかに気持ち悪い使われ方をされても、訴えても勝てるか分からないから諦める」ケースが多発している、というのが今回の問題意識📝

検討会で議論される具体例が生々しく
cases.content:
これは根深い問題。そもそも日本では「肖像権」も「パブリシティ権」も実は法律に明文規定がないんですよね。

長年の判例の積み重ねで確立されてきた権利ですが生成AIによる侵害は事例が新しすぎて、判例がほとんど積み上がっていません。結果として「明らかに気持ち悪い使われ方をされても、訴えても勝てるか分からないから諦める」ケースが多発している、というのが今回の問題意識📝

検討会で議論される具体例が生々しくて、個人的には「これは指針が欲しくなるわ」と感じました。たとえば『声優が演じるアニメキャラクターに、特定の楽曲を無断で歌わせた音源をAIで生成しSNSで収益化』『俳優の顔写真をもとに、その人物が裸になっているかのような画像をAIで生成し公開』といった事例です。どちらも「本人は一切関与していないのに、本人の顔や声として世に流通し、他人が収益を得る」という構造で、常識的に考えて相当アウトに見えます。しかし法的にアウトと言い切れる根拠が弱いまま、野放しに近い状態になっていました。

今後どうなるか事業サイドとして気にせざるを得ない思案かなと。

cases.content:
「嫌中」動画 AIで量産の現場 「軽い気持ちで」男性が語った後悔 ：朝日新聞    
ネットでバズる「新幹線に世界震撼」「桜荒らす中国人」みたいな過激動画、実はAI生成で匿名発注者の下請けが量産！20代会社員が副業で編集してたけど「知らずに加担して怖くなった」と後悔…。これって氷山の一角？ #AI動画

　 ☟ 5chの反応 ☟ 　
日本の衰退論に反論の嵐。税収・企業内部留保が過去最高なのに衰退？円安効果を指摘する声も。焦点は中国擁護論「人口多いから悪い人多いは仕方ない」→「なら良いニュースも日本の10倍溢れてないとおかしいよね？」で一刀両断。良い事例（EV勝ち、ロケット増加）は日本メディアでスルーされがち。中国人の良い人もいるが、企業スパイリスクを警戒する意見多数。輸出依存低く日本は既に「鎖国」並み。

cases.content:
【副業リアルランキング2026年】
AI副業を100件以上リサーチして分かった「稼げた副業・稼げなかった副業」のランキングです。

❌ 稼げなかった副業たち
YouTube：0円
（編集の壁で99%が脱落）
趣味note：0円
（書いて満足で終わる典型）
アンケート系：120円
LINEスタンプ：350円
Googleアドセンス（ブログ）：1,400円
ポイ活：5,200円
Amazonアフィ（雑記）：8,300円
エキストラ：9,000円/日
治験：2万円
AI画像生成の受託：4万円（単発）
AIリライト系の小案件：1.2万円/月
AIチャットボット制作：6.8万円（スポット）

✅ 成功できた副業たち

AI × 動画編集代行：月15〜25万円
AI × SEOリライト：月30万円
AI × 仮想通貨 自動売買：480万円
※ただし損失リスクも同等にあります
AIエンジニア副業：540万円
（専門スキルが前提の上位事例）
法人向けAIコンサル：年670万円
AI × X × note：880万円
（インフルエンサー規模の上位事例）

結論、AIは確かに「桁」を変えてくれます。
でも注目してほしいのは
稼げた副業に共通するポイントです。
特にX × noteは
・労働に依存しない
・資産性が積みあがる
・AIで自動化・量産化できる
という3つが重なっています。
作業時間が減りながら収入が伸びる、というのはこの組み合わせだけです。
コンサルは収入は大きくなりやすいですが、時間を売るモデルなので
どこかで伸び止まりが来ます。

副業で稼げるかどうか、これからはAIを使えるかどうかでほぼ決まってきます。
むずかしく考えなくて大丈夫です。
まずはAIに慣れることから始めてみてください。

cases.content:
Grok君の考察
43秒間シンキングしました

まとめ
この一連の投稿は、単なる「表示が少ない」「カウント回らない」問題ではなく、アルゴリズムのAI排除方針＋視聴者心理の文化的差異＋プラットフォームルールの限界が複合的に絡んだ、2026年YouTubeにおけるAI音楽クリエイターのリアルな苦境を浮き彫りにしています。
 ゼクトさんが自作プレーヤーで「公式の枠外」で戦っているのも、そうした閉塞感に対する実践的な抵抗そのものです。純粋AI生成音楽だけで勝負するのは、ますます難しくなる時代——でも、こうした実験精神こそが、次の突破口を生むのかもしれませんね。

「ゼクトラボ製YouTube動画プレーヤー.js v0.7.10」
2026年現在のYouTube環境を踏まえて深く考察します。
1. カスタムプレーヤー.jsの技術的
・戦略的意義とリスクまず直前の投稿で触れられている**自作JSプレーヤー（v0.7.10）**が核心です。公式YouTube埋め込みを改造し、広告挿入スクリプトをブロック
視聴完了イベントやカウント用トラッキングを意図的にバイパスという「裏技」挙動を実現しています。

これにより広告ゼロ＋公式再生数ゼロという快適体験が生まれますが、YouTubeの公式プレイヤー以外（特にカスタムJSやWebView）では、視聴時間が正しく蓄積されにくく、ビューカウントが無効化されやすい仕組みが知られています。

メリットは「本当の聴取回数」をクリエイター側で独自計測できる点ですが、デメリットは深刻です：YouTube ToSのグレーゾーン（大量利用でBANリスク）
公式アルゴリズムに「人気がない」と判断される
収益化完全無効化

これは単なる便利ツールではなく、プラットフォームのルールに縛られず「自分の戦場」を作る、ゼクトさんらしい実験精神の表れと言えます。
ただし、公式指標が一切上がらないため、長期的にチャンネル成長を自ら阻害する「諸刃の剣」でもあります。

2. AI製音楽に対するYouTubeアルゴリズムの構造的抑圧（2026年現在）インプレッション「0～100/日（通常の1/10000～1/100）」、CTR「1％（通常の1/20～1/6）」という数字は、表示の問題を超えた積極的なアルゴリズム抑圧です。
2026年、YouTube CEOが「低品質AI slop（大量生産・低努力AIコンテンツ）」排除を優先課題に明言し、AI生成コンテンツのスパム対策を強化しています。
判定基準は以下の通り：大量同一パターン生成
視聴維持率（retention）の低さ
エンゲージメント（コメント・いいね）の欠如
人間味の薄い静的ビジュアルや無編集プレイリスト

特にAI音楽は「著作権問題」「ユーザー疲弊」「低品質大量生産」の象徴として標的になりやすく、2月頃にはアニメーション/AI系チャンネルで一夜にしてインプレッションが100万→1.4万に激減した事例が複数報告されています。

CTR1%という異常値は、アルゴリズムが「このコンテンツはユーザーが本気で求めていない」と学習した結果です。単なる「おすすめに出ない」ではなく、検索結果すらほぼ出ない「ソフトシャドウバン」状態に陥っていると言えます。

3. 日外オーディエンスの消費態度の根本的二極化投稿の核心「3000回くらい回ってるのは外人（曲として聞いてる）、日本人はエンタメ動画としか見てない」という指摘は非常に鋭いです。
これは表示以前の心理・文化的ミスマッチです。
外国人（主に海外リスナー）：AI生成音楽を「新しいツールで作られた純粋な音楽」として消費。プレイリスト登録やBGM利用が多く、長時間視聴→好シグナル。特定のジャンル（chill/electronic/experimental）で需要が高い。
日本人：新奇性や「AIでこんなの作ったwww」というメタ的面白さでクリックするエンタメ・ネタ消費。短時間離脱が多く、視聴維持率が悪化→アルゴリズム評価急落の悪循環。
この二極化が致命的です。日本市場のメイン層から好シグナルが得られないため、全体評価が下がり、外人からの「本物の音楽ファン」再生だけが細々と残る構造になっています。
表示オフ設定はここに拍車をかけ、「一過性のネタ」で終わらせる視聴者をさらに増やしています。

4. AI音楽クリエイターの持続可能性と今後の展望この状況は構造的ジレンマです。公式推薦に乗らない → 再生数伸びない → さらに推薦されにくくなる
カスタムプレーヤーを使っても公式指標ゼロ → モチベーション・スポンサー獲得困難

2026年現在、YouTubeはAIコンテンツにラベル義務や検知技術（C2PA/SynthIDなど）を強化しており、純粋AI生成だけではますます厳しくなるでしょう。
生き残る道は：人間編集・声入れ・ストーリー性を加えたハイブリッド型へ移行
超ニッチジャンルで差別化
自前プラットフォーム（Bandcampなど）との併用

cases.content:
## ジュキヤ（Jukiya）の現在地
かつてYouTube界を席巻した**ジュキヤ**さん。メインチャンネルのBANという大きな転換点を経て、現在は主戦場をDMMプラットフォーム（オンラインサロン・ファンクラブ形式）へと移し、驚異的な収益を上げていると言われています。
### 現在の活動と収益状況
YouTube時代の「街頭インタビュー」という武器を、より制限の少ないクローズドな環境へと最適化させたのが現在のスタイルです。
 * **DMMオンラインサロンの成功:** 月額制コンテンツにおいてトップクラスの会員数を抱えているとされ、備考にある「推定月収1億円」という数字も、会員数と単価を照らし合わせれば現実味を帯びる驚異的なスケールです。
 * **「超高級ガールズバー」での豪遊:** SNSや切り抜き動画では、派手な夜遊びやシャンパンタワーなどの様子が散見されます。YouTubeの広告収益に依存しない「直接課金モデル」を確立したことで、かつて以上の資金力を手にしたと言えるでしょう。
### プロフィール・備考
| 項目 | 内容 |
|---|---|
| **主なプラットフォーム** | DMM (J-CLUB), SNS (X, Instagram) |
| **知名度ランク** | **A-** （Z世代を中心に圧倒的、地上波露出は少なめ） |
| **全盛期の武器** | 攻めた企画、類まれなワードセンス、街頭インタビュー |
| **現在の立ち位置** | ネット界の「超高収益クリエイター」兼「夜の帝王」 |
### 考察
YouTubeからの追放（BAN）は多くのクリエイターにとって「終わり」を意味しますが、彼はそれを逆手に取り、**「見たい人だけがお金を払って見る」**というクローズドなエコシステムへの移行に成功しました。
規約の厳しいYouTubeという「公園」から、ルール無用の「ナイトクラブ」へ。まさに彼のキャラクターに合致した生存戦略と言えます。ただ、その豪遊ぶりや過激なコンテンツは、常に世論の賛否を呼び続けているのも彼らしい点ですね。

1つは「二度寝防止アプリ」をTikTok経由だけで10万DLまで持っていった人。やったことは毎日ショート動画を量産→視聴者参加型の「起床チャレンジ」ネタ化。ユーザーに「自分もできる！」と思わせるストーリーが連動投稿でバズに直結。ダウンロード動線は必ずコメント固定とプロフィールリンクで徹底導線強化。アプリとUGCの親和性を最大化。
cases.content:
TikTokの最新トレンドで最速で稼いだ個人の事例、2つ紹介する。

1つは「二度寝防止アプリ」をTikTok経由だけで10万DLまで持っていった人。やったことは毎日ショート動画を量産→視聴者参加型の「起床チャレンジ」ネタ化。ユーザーに「自分もできる！」と思わせるストーリーが連動投稿でバズに直結。ダウンロード動線は必ずコメント固定とプロフィールリンクで徹底導線強化。アプリとUGCの親和性を最大化。

もう1個は、生成AIを活用したショート動画自動量産→収益化の実践者。サムネで「秒で分かる刺激」を強調して1日数十本ペース。他人との差はタイトル→音源→説明文まで一貫した世界観。再生数ボーナスと広告収益で先月だけで50万円超え。運用の肝は、伸びてる動画のデータを即座に横展開し、再現性の高い型に落とし込むこと。

TikTokは今、「バズ＝最速収益」になってる。重要なのは、試す→数字を読む→即修正。工夫を回す人だけが数字を取り切れる環境になった。

cases.content:
「Grok × TikTokショート」
これ、極限までサボりながらバズらせる
「ズルい錬金術」です
　
「ショート動画が稼げるのは知ってるけど、ネタ探しも台本作りもめんどくさい…」
そう思って手が止まってる人、正直めちゃくちゃ損してます。
　
努力や根性なんていりません。
ラクして稼ぎたいなら、X（旧Twitter）のAI
「Grok（グロック）」を使ってください。
　
Grokの最強の武器。
それは「いま、リアルタイムで話題になっていること」を把握する能力です。
これを利用して、以下の3ステップをやるだけ。
　
① Grokに「今日Xで一番、賛否両論になってるトレンドの話題を教えて」と聞く。
② その話題について「TikTokでバズる60秒の台本を作って。冒頭1秒で惹きつける強烈なフックを入れて」と指示する。
③ できた台本を、VrewやCapCutなどの動画生成ツールにコピペして自動生成する。
　
はい、これだけで「今一番バズりやすいトレンド動画」の完成です。
　
自分でゼロから考えてたら、リサーチと構成だけで何時間もかかっちゃうよね？
でもこの方法なら、あなたはAIの作業を「監督」するだけ。
圧倒的にサボりながら、再生回数を稼いでアフィリエイトやnote販売にアクセスを流し込めます。
　
「特別なスキルがないと稼げない」
そんなマインドブロックは今すぐ捨ててOK。
凡人でも、知識がなくても、「勝てるヤツは知ってるヤツ」なんです。
　
サボるために全力を出そう。
　
忘れないように右下のマークから「保存」して、今日からAIをコキ使ってね

全部AIに出させればOK。

・〇〇不足のサイン3つ
・やりがちなNG習慣
・実は逆効果な行動

このへん無限に出てきます。

大事なのは
「1動画＝1テーマ」だけ。

ーーー

③ 伸びる構成はほぼ固定

・冒頭3秒
「これ当てはまってたら注意かも」

・サクッと事実を3つ

・最後
「意外と知られてません」

これだけでOK。

強く言い切りすぎないのがコツ。
医療っぽさは出さずに『グレー』を攻める。

ーーー

④ 編集はむしろ手抜きでいい

・テロップメイン
・背景はフリー素材かAI
・15〜30秒で終わり

作り込みすぎると確実に続かない。

『量産できる形』が正解です。

ーーー

⑤ 稼ぎ方はあとからでいい

・健康食品
・サプリ
・生活改善系サービス

ここで収益化できるけど、
最初から売りにいく必要なし。

まずは
「この人の動画、なんか見ちゃう」
この状態を作る。

信頼 → 収益。順番がすべて。

ーーー

⑥ 最初にやるべきこと

・テーマはブレさせない
・毎日1本
・まずは30本

健康ジャンルは『積み上げゲー』

途中で変える人から消えていきます。

ーーー

健康ショートって

・ずっと需要ある
・誰でもターゲットになる
・単価もそこそこ高い

だから初心者でも普通に戦えます。

cases.content:
there is an uncomfortable truth no one's talking about when it comes to making money with faceless YouTube

you probably won't make any money just learning how to pump out AI voiceover Shorts

you can spend weeks learning CapCut, testing ElevenLabs voices, perfecting your editing flow

but you'll still be broke and there's 1 reason why

you forgot to learn MONETISATION ARCHITECTURE

everyone thinks you can just slap an AI voiceover on a scary fact, upload it and print cash

unfortunately you're wrong

the basics of making money with faceless content is understanding that views alone are poverty wages

the average YouTube Short pays $1–$2 per 1,000 views through the Shorts fund

that means 1 million views = maybe $1,500 if you're lucky

but add a Glitchy CPA link in that same video's description?

one of my students pulled $3,901 from the same million views

same content. same upload. same 15 minutes of work.

the difference isn't the AI. it's not the voiceover quality. it's not the niche.

it's how many revenue layers you stack underneath the content

most faceless creators are running a highway with no toll booths

are you collecting YouTube ad revenue? are you running CPA affiliate links in every description? are you clipping and redistributing across TikTok and Reels for additional revenue? are you using the RIGHT Glitchy offers for your niche's demographic?

if you're not doing all 4 you're leaving 60–70% of your earnings on the table

cases.title:
YouTube just crossed $60B in revenue for 2025. $40B from ads. $20B from subscriptions. Both numbers are growing double digits.

The math here tells you everything about how Google actually views its u
cases.content:
YouTube just crossed $60B in revenue for 2025. $40B from ads. $20B from subscriptions. Both numbers are growing double digits.

The math here tells you everything about how Google actually views its users.

2.7 billion people use YouTube every month. 125 million pay for Premium. That’s 4.6% of the user base generating a third of total revenue. Google’s own executives said on their Q4 earnings call that a Premium subscriber generates “meaningfully higher gross profit” than an ad-supported user.

So YouTube’s real optimization function is straightforward: make the ad experience painful enough that the 4.6% who can afford $14/month convert to Premium, while keeping it tolerable enough that the other 95.4% still watch long enough for advertisers to extract value.

This is a toll booth that charges both directions. Advertisers pay to get in front of you. You pay to make them go away. And YouTube takes a cut of both transactions on the same piece of content, from the same viewer session, off the same infrastructure.

The subscription business alone is now generating ~$20B annually across Premium, YouTube Music, and YouTube TV. That’s bigger than Spotify’s entire revenue. And it grew 25% year over year, adding roughly 2 million new paying subscribers per month through 2024.

The advertisers aren’t getting scammed either. YouTube controls 12.4% of total TV viewing time in the US. Shorts pulls 70 billion daily views. The ad side grew 9% to $40B. Both sides of the market are expanding because YouTube is the only platform where the product being sold to advertisers (your attention) is the same product being sold back to you (your attention, uninterrupted).

That’s a $60B business built on one insight: attention is the only asset you can sell twice.

cases.title:
Engaged RPM vs. Clear RPM

Here's how many people get tricked by how much Shorts channels ACTUALLY make:

Let's start with the definition of Views vs. Engaged Views, because it will unlock the dark tr
cases.content:
Engaged RPM vs. Clear RPM

Here's how many people get tricked by how much Shorts channels ACTUALLY make:

Let's start with the definition of Views vs. Engaged Views, because it will unlock the dark truth of real RPMs.

Views...
... are calculated the moment the Short starts playing.
If a user scrolls past it or it loops, it counts as a view.

Engaged Views...
... are calculated when a viewer watches for a specific threshold (around 2–3 seconds) without immediately swiping away.

When you open someone's channel and see 50,000,000 views - that's JUST Views.

The typical Engaged Views ratio is about 45-50%.

So their 50m is only 22,500,000 Engaged Views.

For example, one of my channels got 404,000,000 Views in the last 90 days, and 196,000,000 were engaged (48%).
Another channel got 196m views with 103 engaged (52%)

Now, what about RPM?

RPM is calculated from Engaged Views (REMEMBER IT), so:

Engaged RPM...
... is what YouTube shows you when you open the "Revenue" tab. It's calculated as:

(Total Revenue) / (Engaged Views) * 1,000

Clear RPM...
... is a metric that you won't find anywhere, but it is the most important number there is. I'll explain why later, for now, try to understand the difference in the formula:

(Total Revenue) / (Total Views) * 1,000

So, for example, a YT Short of mine with 50m views, 25m engaged views, and $4,533 in earnings will look like this:

Engaged RPM = $4,533 / 25,120,000 * 1,000 = $0.17
Clear RPM = $4,533 / 50,100,000 * 1,000 = $0.09

And it creates a problem...

When you say online, "my RPM is $0.25" what other people do is:

1/ They open your page
2/ See 10m views
3/ Assume you made $2,500, while in reality you only made $1,100-1,200

This is because the views they see on your page and the views that were actually calculated are different numbers.

And here's the thing:

You can NOT affect your Engaged Views.

Based on 5b views I generated in the last few months, and looking across multiple channels, I can see that there's no way for you to artificially increase the engaged-to-total views ratio.

It's always 45-55% and you don't have any "tools" to change it.

Which brings me to another point:

Engaged Views and Engaged RPM are absolutely useless for the outside world.

They are only seen inside of your YouTube Studio and required for deep audience research, 

But not for a public eye, because it's confusing.

I don't know a single creator who remembers the number of Engaged Views they generated per month.

However, everyone knows their Total Views and Total Revenue.

That means that we should all switch to using TOTAL (so called Clear) RPM as our main metric.

Otherwise, we create an illusion for an outside viewer that we make twice as much money as what we actually do.

Retweet this so more people understand the difference.

cases.title:
YouTube Payout (Monetization Rates in 2025)

YouTube primarily pays creators through ad revenue via the YouTube Partner Program. Creators get 55% of the ad revenue, while YouTube keeps 45%.Average ear
cases.content:
YouTube Payout (Monetization Rates in 2025)

YouTube primarily pays creators through ad revenue via the YouTube Partner Program. Creators get 55% of the ad revenue, while YouTube keeps 45%.Average earnings: $5–$15 per 1,000 monetized views (also called Revenue Per 

Mille or RPM: the actual amount creators earn per 1,000 views after YouTube's cut).
This equates to roughly $0.005–$0.015 per view.
For 1 million views, expect $5,000–$15,000 (though it can range from $2,000–$10,000 depending on factors).

Key factors influencing payout: Niche (e.g., finance/tech/education often higher, entertainment lower).
Audience location (higher for US/UK viewers).
Ad types and engagement.
Additional revenue: YouTube Premium, Super Chats, channel memberships, etc.

Shorts pay much less: ~$0.01–$0.06 per 1,000 views.

X (formerly Twitter) Payout (Monetization Rates in 2025)

X's main creator program is Ads Revenue Sharing (now heavily tied to engagement from X Premium subscribers). Payouts shifted in late 2024 to focus more on Premium user interactions (replies, likes, reposts) rather than pure ad impressions.

Average earnings: $8–$20 per million verified/Premium impressions (or equivalent engagement).
This is roughly $0.008–$0.020 per 1,000 impressions.
Only impressions/engagements from Premium (paid) users count significantly.
Real creator reports: Often low (pennies to hundreds per month for mid-sized accounts), but high-engagement viral accounts can earn thousands.

Eligibility: X Premium subscription, 500+ followers, 5M+ impressions in recent months.Other options: Subscriptions (fans pay monthly for exclusive content), tips, etc., but ad/share is the primary "payout" similar to views.

Comparison

YouTube generally pays more reliably and higher per view/impression for video content creators, especially with consistent long-form videos.
X payouts are more variable, lower on average, and depend heavily on having an audience of Premium users. It's better for text/thread-based or quick-engagement content, but not as lucrative for most creators compared to YouTube.

Payouts on both platforms vary widely based on niche, audience, and engagement, no fixed rate. These are averages from 2025 creator reports and analytics data.

cases.content:
Even with 2 billion total views, if most are Shorts, the realistic RPM in India is roughly ₹2–₹8 per 1,000 views (often lower). That puts total YouTube Shorts revenue in the range of ₹40 lakh to ₹1.6 crore over time.

If a portion came from long-form videos with a decent RPM of ₹40–₹80, even then total earnings would likely stay under ₹2–3 crore unless supported by brand deals, licensing, or external platforms.

So claims of ₹30–₹38 crore from YouTube views alone don’t add up.

cases.content:

Estimates from analytics, reports, and her disclosures paint a clear picture of a major windfall.

Her output: ~35 podcast episodes, 10M+ YouTube views on the "Charlie's Betrayal" series alone, plus shorts/lives. Baseline pre-death: ~300K podcast downloads/month; post-death: 500K-750K, with 430K new YouTube subscribers in the first week.

YouTube Ad Revenue & Super Chats

- Kirk-series views: 10M+ (top episodes 1-6M each).
- RPM: $5-15/1,000 views (Media Matters 2025 benchmark for right-wing pods; mid: $10).
- Gross ads: $100K-$150K.
- Super Chats: Rumor of $130K in one month debunked by Owens (her screenshot shows $15K/month average); but spikes during Kirk lives added $50K-$80K total.

Podcast & Sponsorships

- Candace Podcast: 35 Kirk episodes (2-3 hrs each) at $10-20 CPM sponsors (e.g., MyPillow).
- Revenue: $500K-$800K (9M+ follower growth in 2025 boosted rates 20-30%).

Substack, Merch, & Donations

- Substack: ~50K new paid subs ($10/month) from "deep dives": $300K-$500K in 3 months.
- Merch/Patreon: 40% sales uptick (books, "Truth-Seeker" tees) + $50-100 pledges: $150K-$300K.

Total Estimate (for the existing multi-millionaire):

$1.1M-$1.8M from Kirk content.

It's a "grift"; Owens denies, but the subscriber surge proves the profit motive.

1. Find
cases.content:
If someone in your family is an expert in ANY niche…

You’re sitting on a potential $10K/month content machine.

Here’s exactly how to turn their knowledge into income in only 10 mins a day:

1. Find the questions people desperately want answered.
 Go on Reddit, Quora, niche forums, and type in keywords related to the niche. 

Then make a list of the most common questions people ask experts in that field.

2. Record simple Q&A videos on your phone.
 Film them in their real environment (office, lab, workspace, clinic).

Ask them the best questions you pulled from your research.

Make the videos longer than 60 seconds so you can earn more revenue on socials. 

3. Post everywhere.
 Upload the same videos to: TikTok, YouTube Shorts, Instagram, Facebook, Snapchat and X.
 The more platforms, the faster the brand grows.

4. Build authority.
 Reply to comments and answer follow-up questions to turn your audience into a community.
 People LOVE getting expert advice for free.

5. Start selling digital products in the niche.
 Examples:
 - A neurosurgeon sells brain longevity guides. - A nutritionist sells customized meal plans. -A therapist sells anxiety toolkits.

Sell products based on what your audience needs help with the most. 

6. Plug your products inside the content.
 Mention it in videos, your bio, your pinned comments and your stories.
 This alone can scale to $5K–$15K/month.

7. Expand when the audience grows.
 Introduce long-form content.
 Offer consulting, high-ticket programs, or even drive traffic to the expert’s private practice.

Then you’ll be able to 10x your earnings.

cases.content: This list may look impressive, but it isn’t fully accurate since YouTube income is not a fixed monthly salary—it varies widely based on views, CPM (which depends on audience location), sponsorships, and external businesses. Creators like MrBeast earn a large share from brand deals and ventures beyond ads, while PewDiePie sees fluctuating income due to reduced activity; similarly, Indian creators such as Dhruv Rathee and CarryMinati depend heavily on sponsorships and CPM differences. In reality, figures like “₹85 crore per month” are mostly rough estimates, and exact rankings are almost impossible because earnings come from a mix of ads, sponsorships, business ventures, and audience demographics.

cases.title: The YouTube video "If You Buy a CYBERCAB How Much MONEY Could You Make?" (link: https://t.co/6DEzxjl5Jq), uploaded on February 21, 2026, by the channel Brighter with Herbert (featuring guest Cern Bash
cases.content:
The YouTube video "If You Buy a CYBERCAB How Much MONEY Could You Make?" (link: https://t.co/6DEzxjl5Jq), uploaded on February 21, 2026, by the channel Brighter with Herbert (featuring guest Cern Basher, a chartered financial analyst from BrilliantAdvice/@CernBasher on X), is a 1-hour-5-minute interview analyzing the potential passive income from personally owning a Tesla Cybercab ($30,000 or less purchase price, as confirmed by Elon Musk for deliveries before 2027) and adding it to Tesla's Robotaxi network.

Key model highlights include: Tesla takes ~35% of fares (owner keeps ~65%), baseline pricing ~$1/mile, 30–65% utilization scenarios, average speed 25 mph, 40% deadhead miles, low operating costs ($0.20–$0.30/mile including electricity, tires, FSD ~$199/month, etc.), yielding optimistic owner gross profits of ~$14,000–$41,000/year before taxes (net ~$8,000–$26,000/year after ~30% tax rate), potentially paying off the vehicle in 1–4 years depending on location/demand, with higher upside in dense urban ($1.50/mile) or rural/long-trip scenarios.

From first-principles thinking, the framework is directionally sound—leveraging EVs' low energy/maintenance costs, removal of driver labor (~60–70% of current ride-hail expenses), and network effects for massive scale—but overly optimistic for the early 2026–2027 rollout phase due to real constraints: bursty demand limiting realistic utilization to 30–45% initially, higher deadhead miles, regulatory hurdles for true unsupervised FSD (Level 4/5, no controls), elevated early insurance/liability/cleaning costs, potential fare compression from competition (Waymo, Zoox, etc.), and owner logistics (not fully passive).

Tesla's strengths—vast real-world FSD data for rapid improvements, vertical integration (e.g., Tesla Insurance, cheap manufacturing), dynamic pricing, and hybrid fleet/individual-owner model—position it to mitigate these over time, potentially delivering the higher earnings at millions-of-vehicles scale in the late 2020s. As of February 22, 2026, with Cybercab production just starting (first unit off the line, initial ramp "agonizingly slow" per Musk), this remains speculative modeling and a proof-of-concept opportunity rather than guaranteed high passive income yet—treat the video's numbers as inspirational upper bounds in dense, high-demand areas once unsupervised autonomy and fleet density prove out.

cases.content:
#BREAKING: YouTube Star #ishowspeed Goes on Hiatus Over "$800K Problem" – Internet Erupts in Debate

🚨 BREAKING: IShowSpeed (30M+ subscribers) just announced he's taking a break from streaming after his monthly earnings dropped to $800,000. He says he feels "undervalued" despite the grind.

And the internet is DIVIDED.

Team Empathy says: "It's about the principle. If your income gets slashed in half because of algorithm changes you can't control, that's a legitimate crisis—whether it's $8K or $800K. These creators run entire businesses with no stability."

Team Reality Check says: "Complaining about $800K/month when people work 3 jobs to afford rent? Peak disconnect. That's more than most people make in 10 YEARS."

Here's what's really going on: Even top-tier streamers are at the mercy of platform algorithms. One update can tank your income overnight. No contracts. No security. Just constant uncertainty.

Industry expert Robert Torres: "If someone with 30 million subscribers can see their income crater, what does that mean for thousands of smaller creators trying to make this a career? This is the canary in the coal mine."

Mid-tier creators are already speaking up: "He just said out loud what we've all been thinking. The platform giveth, and the platform taketh away."

The big questions:
- Should platforms provide more income stability for creators driving their traffic?
- At what point does financial struggle become relative—and does that matter?
- Is the creator economy sustainable or heading for a major correction?

What's your take? Legitimate labor issue or privileged complaint? 

This just pulled back the curtain on an industry millions dream of joining but few truly understand.

💬 Drop your thoughts below 👇

PERKS & PITFALLS OF SHORTS AND LONG FORM VIDEOS

Shorts Perks:

- Monetize quickly (often within a few weeks 4-5weeks)
- beginner friendly skill requirements 
- Simple to automate and scale and more t
cases.content:
PERKS & PITFALLS OF SHORTS AND LONG FORM VIDEOS

Shorts Perks:

- Monetize quickly (often within a few weeks 4-5weeks)
- beginner friendly skill requirements 
- Simple to automate and scale and more time saving too
- you can make many uploads per day (3-4 uploads max)
- Great for idea testing /niche testing 
- Easier to gain initial traction (shorts are most watched in YouTube, the algo can pick up your video & recommend quickly)
- Ideal entry point for new creators
- Generates faster revenues 

PITFALLS 
- Lower earnings per 1,000 views (RPM)
- Shorter channel longevity, typically about a year (but it's not always that rampant)
- Inconsistent performance (people complain about this a lot)
- Heavily reliant on posting volume (4 shorts is okay in a day)
- Higher risk of niche burnout

YouTube Long-Form Content Perks:

- Higher RPM and earning potential
- Stronger personal or brand authority (people will get to know your channel as time goes on)
- Longer-lasting channels
- More predictable income (>5 figs per month for a solid long form channel)

PITFALLS: 
- Takes longer to qualify for monetization (some give up on the way, I did too at some point)
- Requires more advanced skills (video editing and prompting)
- Niche research is more challenging
- Editing is time-consuming and often costly
- Fewer uploads mean slower learning cycles
- Weak titles or thumbnails can ruin strong content

GET TO WORK.

MY CREATOR EARNINGS BREAK DOWN. (And 2026 Roadmap)

Awesome Creator Academy (Community Membership
cases.content:
MY CREATOR EARNINGS BREAK DOWN. (And 2026 Roadmap) 

Partnering with Brands was still roughly 45% of my revenue. (Goal next year is to 2x Brand Collabs) 

Awesome Creator Academy (Community Membership, Coaching, and Cohorts, Mini Courses) was still roughly 35% this year (Goal is to make this 50% of revenue and 3x or 4x) 

Affiliate Links were 10% Or so of revenue. (Goal is to 2x or 3x amount)

The other 10% Was a combination of YouTube Partner Revenue and Book Sales and Misc.

(Goal next year is to 3x or 5x YPP)

I only uploaded 1 video a month on average this year and streamed on average once a month.

Next year I plan to upload 2-3 times a week and stream 2 times per week.

MY 2026 GAME PLAN…

It’s uncommon but I actually WANT to earn more Coaching (and later Consulting Brands/Corps), than from my Content since I make education based content.

I want to earn the majority of my money directly working with Creators and Business owners FIRST… 👊🏾👊🏾

And then Brands and Advertisers Second.

I want the majority of the income to come relationships I earned, instead of reach algorithms ALLOWED me to have.

CONSULTING 

My dream Consulting Clients would be the Atlanta Falcons and the Savannah Bananas if I could get them 🙏🏾🙏🏾😭

But the initial goal is to work with brands and help them not only with content strategy but INFLUENCER MARKETING STANDARDS…

I want to ensure they know how to work with creators, how to pay them , and how to make win/win/win relationships, where the creator and brand put the audience first. 

COURSES

For scale, we’ve never done full courses before and most sold $99 products that are digital asset bundles that include training. 

Our flagship course has been our FREE (pay what you want course).

What I found is that people are ASKING for courses, even my coaching clients, and that the ones they bought from others are outdated, or not helping them with the business and systems to scale (not revenue focused enough, too growth focused or content focused). 

So I’m going to make the product(s) my customers are asking me for, and see what happens. 

What this looks like is 3 courses focused on The Revenue Side of Content Creation and Creator Business Operations (we’ve been building this behind the scenes)

And 2 main courses on Content Strategy and Growth. 

We have 4-6 FREE webinars that we think will outpace most $500 courses and so will the material in the current FREE course, and the $199 Creator Keynote Collection. 

I believe what holds some creators back, is not having a convenient what to know what they don’t know.

And there are just some answers they wouldn’t know to look for on their own even if they were there.

A course solves that. 

CONTENT -

I fell in LOVE with streaming and it’s my favorite way to make content.

I LOVE live production.

But the community struggles with extreme long form and wants and needs 4-20 minute videos and has communicated that.

So I’m going to meet the audience where they are, and I’m actually excited about making this content again. 

I actually have a lot to say (as you know if you watch the live streams).

So I don’t think 2-3 videos a week is unreasonable. 

For weekly live streams, Workshop Wednesdays and Weekend Channel Reviews.

SPONSORED CONTENT.

I love working great sponsors and I think there are really 5 or so companies that are a great fit in the Creator Space and there are at least 25 brands I constant use.

I think Sponsors and affiliates do well when they are organic. 

My strongest collaborations are when I can do UGC for the brand and host for them on their platform or at an event.

COACHING AND COHORTS

Being with creators directly has moved the needle and gotten them the best results possible.

This will be the big focus for me going forward even though it’s the least scalable thing possible.

It’s the most effective direct method to help creators individually.

There are limits to what free corner and paid courses can accomplish. 

1/2

cases.title:
If you have made over $5k a month from YouTube consistently for over 3 months, there is a high chance you will want to go into a high-budget niche. 

This is good. High-budget niches can give stabilit
cases.content:
If you have made over $5k a month from YouTube consistently for over 3 months, there is a high chance you will want to go into a high-budget niche. 

This is good. High-budget niches can give stability. 

But it is also risky jumping into it without knowing what to expect because you believe you have started seeing some money. 

Here is why;

1. A high budget doesn't mean quick and more money in a short time.
It takes time for you to hit good money. 

If you go fully into it, abandoning the other ways to add good income, you will regret it.

2. It requires money. There is no specific range. But you need a top-level research and strategy. You need a good/top writer, editor, etc. They do not come cheaply. 

3. You don't start and immediately get videos hitting that 1million views. It requires patience and an investment of more resources. 

You end up burning your savings and giving up.

My advise:

If you are earning $5k/month, before you go into a high-budget niche, increase your earnings to around $15k/m. 

Get more channels. After 3 months of the above, you will have good savings to start up gradually.

cases.title:
Starting a YouTube channel last year was one of the best things I could’ve done.

Here are the things I’ve learned so far, you can steal them:

1. YouTube is a lot like X

In the sense that you could
cases.content:
Starting a YouTube channel last year was one of the best things I could’ve done.

Here are the things I’ve learned so far, you can steal them:

1. YouTube is a lot like X

In the sense that you could create content for an entire year and not move at all, but 1 upload (1 really good video) will finally kickstart your growth journey.

2. YouTube pays a LOT more

I finally got YouTube monetized this month and I’m bringing in 200K views a month.

I’ve made $1.3K in my first month of being monetized.

For comparison, I bring in 60M impressions a month on X and made ~$5K this month.

For YouTube, that’s about $6.5 per 1,000 views.
While for X, that’s about $0.083 per 1,000 views.

3. YouTube audiences convert to super fans

I’ve been creating content on X for 5 years. Yet, whenever I post a call to action, start a new social media channel elsewhere, conversion is rough.

I’ve noticed that new subscribers on YouTube tend to follow me on X, TikTok, Instagram and everywhere I go.

4. Finding your voice > finding your niche

I constrained myself to 1 niche on YouTube when I started out. And to be honest, that hurt me more than it helped me.

For 11 months I was creating content that 10-50 people were watching.

It wasn’t until that I let go of the imaginary chains of my “niche” that I truly started to grow on YouTube.

And to be honest, the same applies for X and almost every social media app out there.

Closing thoughts:

I’m sure I will learn a lot more in the next few months.

But if I were to tell creators one thing they need to be doing right now, it’d be this:

Never lock yourself under 1 social media channel.

I love X so much, but ONLY building a presence on it is a massive risk. You never know when and how you could lose access to your account.

Spread your presence to 2 main apps.

1 where you put 80% of your time on, and 1 where you put the other 20%.

Most importantly, don’t forget to have fun. :)

I made a living from ONLY making indie games from 2013-2020. I had no YouTube channel, no courses, just games. It's difficult but it is possible. 
 
My SECRET? Keep the cost of living low, keep develo
cases.content:
I made a living from ONLY making indie games from 2013-2020. I had no YouTube channel, no courses, just games. It's difficult but it is possible. 
 
My SECRET? Keep the cost of living low, keep development cycles short, and gain as much experience (and marketing skill) as possible. 
For me living in Portugal I could live a good life on $2000 per month, so if I spend one year working on a game it just needed to make $24k to make a profit. That's a lot more achievable than if I were living in San Francisco and needed $10k per month just to survive and needed $120k to make a profit. 
 
And thankfully my games didn't just get the bare minimum, I hit $1 MILLION in gross revenue in that timeframe across 8 games. 
 
So it's definitely not easy, but it is still possible! 
 
And nowadays the ceiling is basically infinite. The solo dev behind Schedule I made $130,000,000! Now I definitely don't recommend you compare yourself to the extreme outliers, but it's fun to think of what could happen. 
I wish you the best of luck with your games!

cases.title:
If all my YouTube channels got deleted and I had to rebuild to $40K/month in 6 months, here's exactly what I'd do:

• I'd pick a high-RPM niche and go all in. These pay $5-20 per 1,000 views. I wasted
cases.content:
If all my YouTube channels got deleted and I had to rebuild to $40K/month in 6 months, here's exactly what I'd do:

• I'd pick a high-RPM niche and go all in. These pay $5-20 per 1,000 views. I wasted time testing low-RPM niches early on. The difference between $2 RPM and $15 RPM is the difference between struggling and printing money.

• Post 3 videos per week, no matter what. Consistency matters more than perfection. My first 50 videos were mediocre. That's just tuition. You need volume to find what works.

• Use AI for scripts and voiceovers, hire humans for editing. Scripts cost me $30/month with Claude, voiceovers are $10-20/month with ElevenLabs, and I pay editors $70 per video. Total cost per video: $50-70. This is half what it used to cost before AI.

• Research outlier videos before making anything. I look for channels with low subscribers but at least one video with a massive amount of views. That's a proven idea. I model it, don't copy it. This saves months of guessing.

• Hire from Upwork or Fiverr, specifically from Central and Eastern Europe. I've hired over 2,000 freelancers. The talent in countries like Bulgaria, Romania, Hungary is as good as the US but costs way less. 

• Pay freelancers on time and give them $10-50 bonuses when videos hit 100K views. Nobody does this. It keeps the best people working with you forever.

• Once I find a video format that works, I double down hard. I made 9 videos about Jay Leno's garage once the first one hit. 6-7 of them worked. That's how revenue compounds.

• Track RPM, CTR, and average view duration on every single video. If you don't know these numbers, you're flying blind. I spend 1 hour per week analyzing performance.

• Launch channel two once channel one hits $5K/month. Same system, different niche. Going from one channel to multiple channels is how you scale from $10K to $40K/month.

• Build for sale value from day one. Channels sell for 24-36x monthly profit. I sold one channel for over $100K after it made $100K in year one. Most people don't even know this is possible.

• Use a simple Trello board to manage everything. One board per channel. Freelancers move tasks through the stages themselves. Takes me 15 minutes per day to manage multiple channels.

• After 50-100 videos, cut what isn't working. If a format hasn't hit by then, move to a different angle. Don't fall in love with ideas that don't work.

• Focus on the 20% of videos that generate 80% of revenue. Most videos will be okay or flop completely. A few will explode. Find those formats and make more of them.

That's how I went from one channel making $10K/month to multiple channels making $40K/month while working 5-10 hours per week.

Took about 2 years once I figured out the system.

DM me "BLUEPRINT" if you want the full breakdown of how we built this.

cases.content:
I was making $10k/month designing thumbnails for YouTubers, but last month I quit.

Let me explain.

I spent 4 years as a full-time thumbnail designer. Made 1000+ thumbnails, helped creators pull 500M+ views on their channels and generate MILLIONS of dollars.

And if you're reading this post I'm sure you've clicked on one of my thumbnails before.

If you were already following me, you may know me as "Ezekiel", a nickname I chose for myself afraid of being embarrassed using my real name when I started making thumbnails for $5 each.

But that helped me learn how YouTube actually works from the inside. 

So, 2 years ago I started asking myself the obvious question:

"Why am I doing this for everyone else?"

It took me years to build skin in the game, so I finally decided to start my own channel.

January 2024, started my first faceless channel. Surprisingly enough, I managed to earn $15k in 6 months while still making thumbnails full time.

But then, it failed. I was paying more to make the videos than I was earning from them.

I didn't stop there. I was spending all the money I was earning just to create new channels. 

So I started a few more and failed again. And again.

Then one clicked.

October 2024, I had my 2nd ever monetized channel. 

Built it for a year, made $70k from it, and sold it 2 months ago for another $70k. 

All while still making thumbnails for my clients.

But last month I quit thumbnails completely and went all in.

After selling my best channel I had nothing running. I was making $10k consistent monthly income with an easy and flexible job. 

3 years ago I was earning $1,000 per month and I was happy with that.

I could've kept my thumbnail business and still start other channels on the side. But I didn't want that. I wanted to go all in. So I did.

20th January 2026 -> started 4 new faceless channels

Two of them failed. 

But the other 2 made $13k combined in February.

I know these are rookie numbers compared to most YouTube guys on X. 

But I'm not pretending to be someone important here.

I'm just a kid who started making thumbnails for $5 four years ago, and I'm just now getting my head above the water.

My goal is to start at least 20 more channels by the summer and I'm sharing my entire journey here in real time. 

I'm far from being an expert on this.

But I believe that you only have to be 10% better than your peers to be able to teach them something, rather than being an expert who can't even remember what it's like to be at the start anymore.

So if you wanna watch me build to $50k/mo by the end of 2026, follow me.

cases.title:
Everyone keeps tagging me in this conversation, so I guess I'll weigh in:

As an underground dissident artist who has been posting my animations online for 2 decades, and who has been openly Far-Right
cases.content:
Everyone keeps tagging me in this conversation, so I guess I'll weigh in:

As an underground dissident artist who has been posting my animations online for 2 decades, and who has been openly Far-Right since 2016, I've been deplatformed from every way to generate revenue from my work over and over again- the most recent being last year, when half my income got wiped out overnight.

It's only been less than a year that I've been allowed to get back on mainstream platforms and start making some income from my animations again, which I'm very grateful to be able to do.

In the two years since returning to Twitter after my 2017 ban, I've gone from essentially being a dead girl with my footprint completely hijacked and erased, to being one of the top posters on RW X, and accumulating a massive audience who respects and loves me and my work.

I went from the bleak hell of being confined to an app-banned Telegram and Bitchute channel, to getting articles in The Spectator, Atlantic, mentions on CNBC and CSPAN, and praise from multiple Billionaires. All in a two year span of being allowed to operate back amongst the living. 

The Will Stancil Show is acknowledged far and wide as one of the most successful modern pieces of Right Wing Art and Political Satire, as well as making technological history for being the first AI-Animated TV Series. And I did it completely on my own, with zero help or backing - a level of independent success that is increasingly rare.

Despite this success, however, the only financial compensation I am receiving for this work are small, one-off donations by my fans, and creator fees from a low market cap meme coin. If I were to only count the amount that I am making per month just from this show, it would barely be enough for me to survive on a poverty budget.

I am extremely grateful to all the regular people who have made these donations to me, and ofcourse to Andrew Torba who has recently granted me unlimited credits on @Gab__AI - which were previously costing me $250-800 per episode. Torba's patronage is truly exemplary, and if anyone wants an example of a RW Patron, it's him.

But despite praise from some of the tech elite and billionaires, and despite being one of the few to make a watershed demonstration of AI's capabilities, I have not received one offer to fund the show from anyone with a significant amount of money. We all know why this is: My work and political opinions are simply still too politically incorrect, even with this right wing vibe shift. 

Even if I was to get an offer, they always come with a price, especially on the Right. Any significant offers would no doubt be made on the condition that I insert certain political rhetoric or relinquish ownership of my work. And as someone who fiercely values my total independence, political integrity, and creative control above all else, this would not be something I would ever do under any circumstance.

So I am just in a funny little situation here. The elites see me, they're watching my show, and they know that I see them too. But they know they can't touch me and I can't touch them. Because at the end of the day, even amongst the Right, I am still too radical and antisemitic, and I refuse to compromise for anyone.

I will always continue to make The Will Stancil Show regardless of income simply because it is what I love to do best in the world. I have always lived first and foremost for my art, and receiving the AI has increased my love and productivity tenfold. Truly, I am about the happiest that I've ever been, because I can make this work and I have an audience again. Because to me, the glory of being acknowledged as a Great Artist is worth more than any money. And in the end that is the only thing that results in a Great Artist. 

In short: the RW slush money is out there, and the RW Artist and her work is out there, but they won't ever collide. I won't rule out any no-strings-attached patronage in the future, but I also won't hold my breath.

something where I can see 5 competitors under 100K subs already getting consistent high views

then I'd use Claude to write scripts based on their viral topics

repeat the exact same process

if it's an educational channel I'd add a simple digital product

from there just keep scaling with more channels or translate your best channel into other languages

cases.title:
I started Art Of War on January 27th,

A year later:

• 3.8 billion views
• $300,000 made
• 1.5m followers

This channel changed the 3D Shorts niche forever,

Here’s how I did it in 5 stages:

𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗚𝗘 #
cases.content:
I started Art Of War on January 27th,

A year later:

• 3.8 billion views
• $300,000 made
• 1.5m followers

This channel changed the 3D Shorts niche forever,

Here’s how I did it in 5 stages:

𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗚𝗘 #𝟭: 𝗡𝗜𝗖𝗛𝗘

Choosing a niche is a big deal.

I didn’t know that.

I just wanted something that no one else was doing.

I saw how Zack D Films was doing 3 billion views while others were fighting for pennies in the commentary niche.

I instantly knew the game I wanted to play.

I took Zack D’s most popular Shorts and figured out that around 40% of them were related to guns and war stories.

It sounded like a cool niche, and I had a great name for it:

“𝗔𝗿𝘁 𝗢𝗳 𝗪𝗮𝗿”

Right after this, I googled “𝘩𝘰𝘸 𝘵𝘰 𝘮𝘢𝘬𝘦 𝘡𝘢𝘤𝘬 𝘋 𝘍𝘪𝘭𝘮𝘴 𝘷𝘪𝘥𝘦𝘰𝘴,” and my search down that rabbit hole began.

It turned out that no one was doing it for a reason.

3D animation is 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲.

I went on Upwork to find out that each video was going to cost me $300-500.

I didn’t want to pay that. It was just crazy.

After negotiations with one of the freelancers, we got to $150 per video + 40% of the revenue.

And that’s where it all began.

𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗚𝗘 #𝟮: 𝗖𝗢𝗠𝗠𝗢𝗡 𝗥𝗢𝗨𝗧𝗘

On January 27th, I posted my first ever video.

I still think it was one of the coolest I’ve ever done.

Maybe because it was the first.

And I started scaling the channel the “common way.”

1st video – 0 views
2nd video – 12k views
3rd–6th videos – 15k views

We were stuck. Dead stuck.

But only until I posted the 7th video.

It blew up to 30m views in 5 days, and all the other videos followed.

The account got monetized and gained 50m views in the next 7 days.

That was a BANGER.

But for the next 3 months, I was stuck.

65% of the revenue went to the freelancer, 30% to taxes,

I was left with 0–5% profit margins, which was impossible to sustain.

I tried hiring more people, but freelancers from Upwork don’t care.

They went to work for competitors for bigger pay, stealing all our systems.

That’s where I see most 3D channels gave up. I was doing 100m views a month, yet was barely making $500 to my name.

I needed to change the route.

𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗚𝗘 #𝟯: 𝗠𝗬 𝗥𝗢𝗨𝗧𝗘

I come from the business world. 

$90k/mo agency at 20. Worked with Sephora. Signed yearly contracts with 9-figure companies. I knew how business works.

So instead of scaling like commentary boys do, I played a different game:

1. I found an investor to sponsor 3 months of salaries (gave him 10%).

2. Hired 5 FULL-TIME animators from CIS countries (f*ck Upwork freelancers).

3. Scaled from 15 videos a month to 60 videos a month in less than 40 days.

And the results went nuts.

April: 103m views
May: 260m views
June: 720m views

We made $50,000 in June, with $40,000 being pure profit.

That was insane.

And the only reason it happened is because I ignored the advice of every guru on Twitter and did my thing.

“Hire on Upwork and OnlineJobs” – f*ck you
“Work with freelancers” – f*ck you
“Use your channel profits to scale” – f*ck you
“Just post better content” – f*ck you x2

I did everything “wrong,” and that’s how we went from nobodies to the most effective 3D team to ever run a short-form channel.

But this was not the end...

𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗚𝗘 #𝟰: 𝗧𝗨𝗥𝗕𝗨𝗟𝗘𝗡𝗖𝗘

Then our views dropped.

We went from 720m views in June to 500m views in July.

Revenue dropped from $50k to $35k.

I thought it was over (lol).

I didn’t know how inconsistent YouTube Shorts are, and I wasn’t used to it back then.

Each drop really hurt me.

Things were turbulent.

But then we did 600m views again.

Did $56k in revenue in October.

Hit our first $𝟰,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗗𝗔𝗬.

It was all just part of the game and something to get used to.

Until November….

𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗚𝗘 #𝟱: 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗙𝗔𝗟𝗟𝗢𝗨𝗧

But then things really turned bad.

Not only for AOW, but for the entire 3D niche.

We saturated our topics so much that the audience couldn’t stand it anymore.

The wave of AI slop hit, and the channel’s views dropped by 70%.

That was a disaster that I’m still figuring out.

We were like a rapper who squeezed everything out of a viral song, but then couldn’t write a new one.

And the only channels who survived this are the ones doing something SO unique, that they practically have no competitors.

Unfortunately, war was not one of those topics.

We’re at the stage where we need to invent a new format, something fresh that the audience actually wants to watch.

Because our systems for getting the most views in the moment worked so well, we oversaturated our own formats and topics.

And this is where we finish our insane 365 days of running Art Of War.

The next year is a year of experiments.

The team is sharper and more creative than ever before.

And we’re onto a generational run in 2026.

We’re exploring long form, dozens of storytelling techniques, hundreds of formats.

And once we find something that works… be ready. Art Of War 2.0 is going to shake the world one more time.

But for now,

Happy Birthday, Art Of War.

The project that changed the 3D world in less than a year.

cases.title: Couple months ago I shared post about helping my friend make a few grand with meta ads. We're now looking at £23K gross in 2.5 months with £2.8K in spend - starting from £0 with iphone videos 2 produc
cases.content:
Couple months ago I shared post about helping my friend make a few grand with meta ads. We're now looking at £23K gross in 2.5 months with £2.8K in spend - starting from £0 with iphone videos 2 products on free shopify template site. 

Outside of initial setup it takes about 5 minutes a week to manage.

 Spend has been £20- £60/day but not more because they don't have the stock. AOV is at £200. 

I've talked to dozens of business owners who already at a decent level but don't want to "risk" £2-£5k to test something. 

I've spent a couple thousand of my own money testing stuff for my friends businesses for free because I believe I can make it repeatable and it's worked pretty well (i'll share some more examples later)

So here's how I like to do it:

We start small - we might need 2-3x ideal your target cpa to test per day so £50-£100 (but sometimes I'll start with £20) - first month is setting up conversion tracking, doing our research and make our first set of assumptions.

I'll either work on meta or google, or both, depending on budget. time/assets etcPer channel, per adset / adgroup

First £1-2K - Just getting data and finding out what DEFINITELY DOESN'T WORK. Sometimes we make a profit day one (once made £1K in an hour of something going live from £0 but who's counting...), sometimes it takes longer, but I mentally prepare to never see this money again.

After about 40 - 50 days we have a good idea of what looks like it might have legs, the structure / setup / funnel / products that are worth persisting with. 

By this point usually I have some clear winning combinations that I just try to pump more money into and improve efficiency - this is where all the channel level stuff comes into it for me, so from negative kw's and bid strats on google, to audience / ad optimisations on meta. 

I think rinse and repeat to build "layers" within an account of scalable campaigns /adsets etc etc. 

The channel level technicalities people fuss and argue about don't mean that much to me.. i don't care. Can I put in more money, reach more people and are they converting, that's pretty much all i care about. 

And I just work up and down asking and answering these questions mentally looking at the data and making decisions that way. I also try to not change too many things too quickly, a lot of the time I'm just watching how people interact with the ads, websites etc and what the platforms are reporting.

cases.title:
My client had 2 million YouTube subscribers.

He was making $2,000/month.

Most people would tell him to post more, optimize his thumbnails, or chase the algorithm.

We did none of that.

Instead, we
cases.content:
My client had 2 million YouTube subscribers.

He was making $2,000/month.

Most people would tell him to post more, optimize his thumbnails, or chase the algorithm.

We did none of that.

Instead, we changed one thing: where his viewers went after watching his videos.

30 days later, he made $15,000.

Here's what actually happened (and why this matters if you're using YouTube to grow your business):

The Problem:

He was relying 100% on YouTube AdSense. 2 million subscribers sounds impressive, but ad revenue is pennies unless you're getting tens of millions of views per month.

He had an audience. He just had no offer for them to buy.

The Solution

We built a simple redirect system. Every video now pointed viewers to his Fortnite creative maps 

something he controlled
something that paid him directly.

No new content strategy. No viral videos. Just a better monetization path.

Result? 

7x revenue increase in one month.

Why This Matters For Your Business:

If you're a coach, consultant, or service provider trying to use YouTube for leads, most of you are making the same mistake my client was:

You're optimizing for views instead of conversions.

You obsess over:

- Views &
- Subscriber count

But you ignore:

- Where viewers go after watching
- What action you're asking them to take
- Whether your content leads to a call, sale, or lead

Here's The Truth

You don't need a million views to build a profitable YouTube channel. You need 1,000-2,000 views from the right people + a clear path to your offer.

My client had millions of views going nowhere. We gave them somewhere to go, and everything changed.

What You Should Do Instead:

Every video should have a purpose. Ask yourself:

"If someone watches this video, what do I want them to do next?"

- Book a call?
- Join your email list?
- Buy your low-ticket offer?
- Download your lead magnet?

Then make it obvious. Pinned comment. End screen. Video description. Verbal CTA.

Bottom Line:

YouTube isn't a social media platform for businesses. It's a lead generation machine.

BUT only if you treat it like one.

STOP CHASING VIEWS...

cases.title:
This past month, I’ve gained over 4,000 subscribers, averaged 100k+ views per short, and accumulated over 3 million views on my channel (and growing!) 

🩸Here’s my advice if you want to pop off in 202
cases.content:
This past month, I’ve gained over 4,000 subscribers, averaged 100k+ views per short, and accumulated over 3 million views on my channel (and growing!) 

🩸Here’s my advice if you want to pop off in 2026:

-

DISCLAIMER: these are what worked for me. Content isn’t one size fits all 

-

DON’T be a vtuber. No, I don’t mean give up the model entirely- but consider making something besides your model the focus of your short form content. Some algos (cough clock app) specifically supress your discoverability BECAUSE of your model- i’ve had 2 accounts shadowbanned because of it. on top of this, some of your potential audience will scroll due to disinterest in vtubing as a topic. Instead, use emotes or memes! Try voice over or scripted content rather than clips! Get your audience interested in YOU and your content! (for storytime content this is different)

START RIGHT NOW. learn to edit, or be prepared to drop alot of money. learning the program is the hardest part, but it gets easier as you go! your future self will thank you.

DO RESEARCH. What do others that are successful within your niche do? What about outside of your niche? After you post, if it flops, don’t blame the algo or get discouraged. My manager once told me “just because it doesn’t perform well doesn’t mean it’s bad content.” Look at your stats- was your hook lacking? video dragging? with every failure, pinpoint 1-3 things you want to do differently next time!

EXPERIMENT! while staying WITHIN YOUR NICHE, try different styles and focuses! see what sticks, and recreate that. for example, my videos with voice overs and voice acting perform on average better than gameplay clips alone. So i shifted my focus to that content, and my average views went from around 50k to over 100k per short! if you venture out of your niche, the algo won’t know who to push your content to- so whatever topic you choose, don’t stray from it.

show PERSONALITY. If you’re doing scripted content, it can be easy to fall monotone. while monotone can be funny, whatever you do make sure you do it INTENTIONALLY. show people what makes you unique, what you have to offer- that’s how you’ll convert them into followers and regular viewers. make something only YOU can make! even if it takes time to figure out what that is, that’s okay. whatever you do, try not to sound like you’re reading off a script.

if you can, GET HELP. Hiring a manager was one of the best decisions i made. at the time, i was deciding between hiring an editor to supplement my shorts while i focused on long form, or a manager to help me with strategy, advice, and accountability. Having someone holding me accountable to my commitments made me go from making excuses to consistently showing up. As a small vtuber, you’re likely your own content planner, editor, manager, and marketer on top of trying to stream. For most, that’s just too much on top of other life responsibilities. So if you really want to do this, get help! Invest in to yourself before you hope for anyone to invest in you. Don’t drop thousands on a nice model- put that into you and your content instead.

Most important is to have fun! If you love what you do, it will show in your content- that’s what will draw people in!

Best of luck to you all! Here’s to no more excuses in 2026- let’s make this our year!! ❤️

cases.content:
I also got hit with inauthentic content on my big channel. $40k from December = most likely gone :(

Channel is doing 240k realtime views, recent vid hit 200k views, another hit 150k views. It hurts, ngl

Some interesting events leading up to this:

- Channel joined CMS (O&O) about a month ago (CMS does not seem immune from inauthentic content)

- Channel was leaked by a guru not so long ago

- Had to copyright strike a Vietnamese guy who uploaded a lot of my videos (all 6 vids were removed by YT, yet his channel is still alive and monetized, WTF) - got hit with inauthentic same day 😅

Some info about the channel:

- All vids made in 1 click using AI / code, proudly built by me (I know this is exactly what they're trying to avoid lol)

- Video length between 2 and 3 hours

- Minimum 40 images per video all shown for varying amounts of time. Once the images "ran out", it would loop all image/videos in the same video until the end (maybe this is no longer good enough?)

- Used AI generated video footage in first minute of all videos since last few weeks

- Channel was considered to be in a "safe" sleep niche

- I recreated many of my outliers, so I had many videos of very similar title/thumbnails. Perhaps this is a bad thing to do since it's too "repetitive"?

- Comments on my videos are overwhelmingly positive, some people used it to fall asleep to and others just found it super interesting

I am just grateful I did make $15k during November and that money is now safely in my bank account. I feel lucky I even got paid at all and that I didn't quit my job yet

My 2 smaller channels are not hit with anything, but they're making peanuts compared to my big channel

cases.title:
Happy New Year! As we move into 2026, I want to reflect on a crazy year for Heretics.

The podcast is right now in its infancy, having started just 2 years and 1 month ago. Year 2 was even better than
cases.content:
Happy New Year! As we move into 2026, I want to reflect on a crazy year for Heretics.

The podcast is right now in its infancy, having started just 2 years and 1 month ago. Year 2 was even better than the first, with 423k new subs taking us to 715k. That means that, each month, more than 35,000 new people not only discovered the channel but aligned with it so much that they stuck around.

It has now been 5.5 years since I first started podcasting. I had been on minimum wage for a decade, doing various online jobs to make ends meet (just about) while living in France, Colombia, Argentina, Brazil & Germany. 

In my spare time, I taught myself to speak five languages and how to make documentaries. I made one about an abusive exorcist - sold it to the BBC for peanuts after two years of calling and emailing hundreds of TV channels - and then was told - despite its huge success - that I'd have to be off-screen due to DEI from then on.

Thus, the very first episode of what was then called On the Edge with Andrew Gold was filmed with a shitty, sellotaped webcam and no microphone, on my laptop sitting on a shoebox. I interviewed Nate Phelps, a member of the Westboro Baptist Church. 

In the first month, I had four listeners. It took a year and a half of non-stop work, along with my other jobs, before the podcast (I mainly focused on Spotify and Apple) reached 10,000 listens per month, allowing me to make enough through ads to give up my other work.

After a conversation with YouTuber Chris Williamson - back before either of us had much presence - he convinced me to focus on YouTube. 

A year and a half later, On the Edge with Andrew Gold had hundreds of thousands of subscribers. It was one of the biggest channels on YouTube (for views) about cults, with Scientology my speciality. I got into live-streaming, and even spent some time exposing the madness of Meghan Markle.

I was making real money for the first time in my life, too, which I put towards a house - a nice change from the mould-infested flat I'd been renting. Soon, I felt that I had said everything I could ever wish to say about Scientology and Meghan Markle! But when I tried to branch out, the channel's audience capture meant the videos sank.

I was becoming increasingly frustrated by what I saw around me - namely, woke madness, the Islamification of Britain, mass immigration and the utter arrogance of our political and media class. But most of my audience was American and not interested in politics.

That's when I decided to gamble everything.

In November 2024, I made my first appearance on Triggernometry and went to the Battle of Ideas, where they let me interview some of their speakers...whose videos I used to launch a brand new channel: Heretics.

I found that such was the appetite for sensible, calm views that ran counter to the mainstream that it blew up. It's not just on YouTube (where I hope we hit 1m subs in 2026). We have recently started posting regular clips on social media. We are already getting 45k+ followers per month, and in no time at all, we've amassed:

Facebook: 189k subs.
Instagram: 146k subs.
X: 134k followers.

But here's the critical part.

As creators and viewers, we have moved the goalposts. After years of being gaslit, people now know about the BBC's inherent bias (even if little is being done about it). And there is a growing appetite for something different. Celebrities get in touch to say they're secret subscribers, and I hope that they'll start moving the Overton Window in the legacy media.

There will be detractors. I wanted this channel to be sensible and compassionate, and this is reflected in the kind of people who come up to me to say hi when I'm out and about. Friendly, normal people. This means, however, that from time to time, I will draw a hard and fast line on views that I consider dangerous.

Yes, mass immigration has been horrific, but no, deporting anyone who is not English is not the answer. Yes, trans is a ridiculous and illiberal concept; but no, I won't advocate for trans people being banned from events or made to feel unwelcome. Still, I won't hold back in addressing these illiberal concepts and rebuking the morons who utterly ruined our country with progressive dogma.

That's just me - and the fact that so many of you keep tuning in means it's also you. At times, it will seem (especially on X) like swarms of people are turning on me and this show. This can be an isolating feeling, especially when friends feel understandably fearful of speaking out - no one wants to get caught up in one of those things. It's unpleasant.

But the viewership and sub count are a truer indicator - and both continue to grow. I can't thank you enough for that. We are on the right path. I couldn't do it without you sensible people who are brave enough to push back against a media and political class that stopped caring about you.

On to 2026: who do you want to see on the show? What were your favourite moments of 2025? Let me know below!

A year of loss, growth, and reinvention

January: Hopeful, crushing it on all my
cases.content:
End of 2024          ➡️          End of 2025

A year of loss, growth, and reinvention

Writing this more so as a journal for myself to look back at next year.

January: Hopeful, crushing it on all my social medias, most in a month I’ve ever made

February: Bought my first car fully in cash

March: Dad gets very sick and passes away very unexpectedly and sudden

April: Hard to focus on anything at this point, but focusing on keeping family close

May: Begin grinding hard on faceless YouTube channels to keep my mind occupied, crushing it just unfulfilling

June: I visit Austin Texas and fall in love

July: I move out for the first time 1100 miles from home into a 1800sqft house all alone to rebuild myself

August: Learning to be independent and get my shit together, I start dating an amazing girl

September: I find myself and my purpose, start hitting the gym more, eating healthier, cleaning myself up, really prioritizing my health after neglecting it all these years

October-November: Just focusing really hard on work and what my purpose in life really is, the most crucial months of my entire life

December: Burnt out on faceless YouTube, even though I was making more per month I was unhappy. As of writing this I sold both and kept a 5% stake at the end of Q4

My real passion has always been making content and being myself, not hiding behind a faceless channel just to earn money.

I fell out of love with my Syinite channels because I started making videos I wasn’t happy with, and doing it for the money, and once I realized I could make more money with faceless + less effort I stopped trying.

I learned a lot in this process with content creation and I’m ready to take it to the next level and go way bigger in a form of content that is true to myself.

I built up 4M followers across multiple niches when I had no idea what I was doing, and didn’t know my purpose, now it’s time to really make a name for myself and this time do it right, authentically, genuinely, and build a community.

I tried this briefly in September and I’ll admit I failed, that’s because I was still trying to do faceless at the same time and it was just to much at the time to handle.

So now I’m currently all in on this, it’s time to give this everything I got again, it’s all or nothing :)

cases.content:
The BORING faceless channels that made me $34,528 in 90 days:

(No virality. No face. No trends.)

Here’s the exact system 🧵👇

Most people do YouTube like this:

• Spend days on an idea
• Hope it goes viral
• Get a spike (maybe)
• Then back to 0

It’s a hamster wheel.

I was stuck in it too.

Then I realised something:

YouTube isn’t just a content platform.

It’s a search engine.

Every day, millions of people are typing:

• “how to fix…”
• “how to set up…”
• “best way to…”

And most of those searches?

Have TERRIBLE videos.

That’s the opportunity.

Instead of chasing views…

You just answer demand.

And every time someone searches -> you get paid.

Here’s the exact model:

Find the search
Make the video
Stack videos
Get paid daily

Simple. But powerful.

Step 1: Find the search

Go to YouTube and type:

"how to ___"

Look at autocomplete.

Those are REAL searches happening right now.

Example:

❌ “Excel tips”
✅ “how to freeze columns in Excel Mac”

Boring = low competition.

3 checks before I make a video:

• Shows in autocomplete
• Existing videos get SOME views
• Evergreen (searched for years)

If it passes -> I make it.

Step 2: Build the asset

This is where most people overcomplicate it.

You don’t need:

• Fancy editing
• High production
• Personality

You just need ONE thing:

A clear answer.

Your video should:

• Match the title EXACTLY to the search
• Answer immediately (no intro fluff)
• Solve the problem fully
• Be 2–4 minutes max

That’s it.

Step 3: Stack the library

This is where it compounds.

20 video = $1-2/day

200 videos = $10-20/day

1000 videos = $50-100/day

Each video = an asset.

And they don’t die like viral content.

I have videos from 2 years ago…

Still getting views every single day.

No uploads needed.

That’s the difference.

The key?

Stay in ONE niche.

Example:

• Accounting software
• Shopify Tips
• App tutorials

You become the “answer channel” for that topic.

And YouTube pushes you harder.

Step 4: Collect the income

This is where it gets interesting.

Entertainment RPM: $1-3

Search niches RPM: $8-25+

Why?

Because the viewers are buyers.

Example:

90K views × $14 RPM = $1,260/month

Now stack multiple videos + channels…

You see how this scales.

The reason most people ignore this?

It looks boring.

Nobody shares it.

Nobody flexes it.

But someone searches for it…

Every. Single. Day.

And every time they do -> you get paid.

That’s the game.

Predictable traffic > viral spikes.

If I had to rebuild from ZERO in 2025…
I’d use this exact path to hit $30k/mo in 1-2 months.

I’d start with what everyone thinks is dead: movie recaps.
AI turned them into a money glitch.

98% of creators don’t even know.

This is your way to $30k/mo on YouTube step by step:

Step 1. Start with traditional movie recaps channel (AI voiceover)
Pick random movies that already exist and make recaps of what happened in the movie.
Easy to produce, easy to upload. Don’t overthink it.

Step 2. Post 30-50 videos (total)
On video ~50 you’ll finally have REAL data.
You’ll see which movies / topics pull views…
…and which ones are stuck at 0.

Step 3. Read the data
Forget your “taste”.
Look at watch time, click-through, views.
Circle the outliers. Those few videos that beat everything else.

Step 4. Switch to YOUR OWN AI movies
Now use those winning topics to write your own stories.
Use AI to generate scripts, voices, visuals.
You’re no longer copying a movie someone else made.
You control the WHOLE thing.

Step 5. Milk the winners HARD
Take the top ideas and make sequels, spin-offs, variations.
Same proven theme, new executions.
This is how you break into millions of views.

Traditional recaps get you monetized fast and give you the data you actually need.

Once you know your outliers, you’re NO longer LIMITED by existing movies, YOU CONTROL the story based on what your audience already proved they want.

A 30min AI movie might cost ~$100 to produce…
but it runs at ~98% profit margins (Revenue: $5k per movie) and stays evergreen forever.
That’s long-term passive income stacked ON TOP of your scaling.

Recaps are your testing ground.
AI movies are where you scale.

Follow this path and $10k/mo stops being luck and starts being math.

cases.title: 🦔 AI-generated content has flooded social media to the point that users are calling it "slop" and a backlash is growing. According to research from AI company Kapwing, 20% of content shown to a fresh
cases.content:
🦔 AI-generated content has flooded social media to the point that users are calling it "slop" and a backlash is growing. According to research from AI company Kapwing, 20% of content shown to a fresh YouTube account is now low-quality AI video. On YouTube Shorts, 104 of the first 500 clips shown to a new account were AI-generated. The top AI slop channel has 2 billion views and earns an estimated $4 million annually.

Meta's Mark Zuckerberg called this social media's "third phase" and said the company is leaning further into AI content creation. YouTube's CEO acknowledged concerns about "low-quality content, aka AI slop" but ruled out making judgments on what should and shouldn't be allowed.
Pinterest users became so frustrated that the company introduced an opt-out system for AI content. Under many viral AI videos, comments calling out the fake content now get more likes than the original posts.

My Take
I've noticed this on every platform. The feeds are filling up with obviously fake heartwarming content, weird AI cartoons, and videos designed to provoke engagement rather than inform or entertain. AI makes content creation nearly free, platforms reward engagement regardless of quality, and creators chase views because that's where the money is.

The social media companies aren't going to fix this because they don't see it as broken. Zuckerberg is celebrating AI content as an expansion of what Meta can monetize. YouTube says they won't make judgments on quality. The platforms make money whether the video is real or fake, as long as you keep scrolling. One researcher put it well: over time, people will stop trying to verify what's real because it takes mental effort. We just consume faster and think less. That's the product now.

Hedgie🤗

Someone comments a keyword on a video, they get a message that feels personal

Three real tips. Fourth tip is the product with a link

Women 35-55 didn't grow up terminally online. They don't know what AI content looks like. They just see a nice person giving good advice

That trust gap has maybe 12-18 months left

AI detection will improve. Platform policies will tighten. Audiences will get smarter

The people who build their farms NOW will have established audiences, established revenue, and established trust before the window closes

he built 3 AI characters using tools that cost him $60/month. a grandmother. a monk. a healer. they look real. sound real. post short videos every day about health and wellness

the audience is women 35-55 who think these characters are actual people sharing wisdom from their kitchen

that's it. that's the whole thing

no degree required
no experience required
no camera required
no money required beyond $60
no employees
no inventory
no ad spend

now compare that to every "legitimate" path your parents told you about:

doctor: 8-12 years of training. $200-400K in debt. doesn't start earning real money until 30-33. net worth doesn't go positive until 40

lawyer: passes bar at 25. $150-250K in debt. works 60-70 hour weeks. won't clear $200K until they make partner which takes 7-10 years and has a lower acceptance rate than med school

both made the same bet: trade your entire 20s for the PROMISE of money in your 30s

and for most of them that promise quietly underdelivers once you subtract debt servicing, malpractice insurance, and the decade of compound interest they missed while their net worth was negative

meanwhile Ollie followed a step-by-step system for 4 months and now makes more per month than most people his age make in 3

and he's not even close to the ceiling. mfs in our network are clearing $30-50K/month. one account hit $641,000 in GMV in a single month

the uncomfortable part is WHY this works

Ollie's method doesn't select for intelligence. doesn't reward credentials. doesn't require anything your guidance counselor told you matters

1. finding products ALREADY selling (free data, takes 20 minutes)
2. building an AI character that matches the buyer (takes 20 minutes)
3. copying proven viral scripts and adapting them (takes 30 minutes)

the audience doesn't give a shit about your face. they care if the content helps them and if the person delivering it seems trustworthy. consistency builds trust. not jawlines

i'm not saying don't become a doctor. if medicine is your calling go save lives

but if you chose that path bc you thought it was the only reliable route to financial freedom the math changed permanently while you were in residency

and every month you spend debating whether to start is a month Ollie's pages are compounding. his videos from January still drive revenue in April. every piece of content makes every previous piece more valuable

a medical degree takes 8-12 years and $200K+. this takes $60/month and a few hours a day

the math isn't ambiguous. run the numbers

product selection, character creation, scripting, video generation, all of it

run by 12 operators who've done $70M+ in tracked GMV… people who've attended are hitting $10-30k/mo within their first few months. no face, no experience, no following needed

comment "APEX" and i'll dm you the link. it's free
